does: PrivacyLens introduces a framework for evaluating privacy norm awareness in language models deployed in real-world communication scenarios like email and social media. The approach extends privacy-sensitive seed cases into multi-level evaluations: expressive vignettes that contextualize privacy concerns and agent trajectories that simulate realistic LM behavior in interactive settings. The framework grounds evaluation in privacy norms derived from privacy literature and crowdsourced data, revealing a critical gap between how LMs answer direct privacy questions and how they actually behave when following user instructions in practice. This multi-level methodology captures the contextual and long-tailed nature of privacy violations that traditional evaluations miss.
connects: This work addresses limitations in existing privacy evaluation approaches for language models, which typically rely on simple probing questions disconnected from realistic deployment contexts. It builds on prior research in LM safety and alignment that examines model behavior in specialized domains, extending these methods to the privacy dimension. The paper connects to broader work on privacy-aware AI systems and fairness evaluation frameworks that use synthetic scenarios and human crowdsourced judgments to assess model behavior. It responds to growing concerns about LM agents' agency in personalized communication, building on earlier work examining privacy risks in neural text generation and the contextual nature of privacy norms across different communication scenarios.
enables: PrivacyLens establishes infrastructure for developing and deploying privacy-aware language models at scale. The framework enables future work on training methods that align LM behavior with contextual privacy norms rather than superficial privacy understanding. It points toward building privacy-aware reward models or fine-tuning approaches that leverage the multi-level evaluation structure. The work suggests directions for automated privacy norm discovery across cultural and contextual boundaries, as well as techniques for detecting and mitigating privacy violations in LM-mediated communication systems. This foundation could support development of privacy-by-design practices for conversational AI and inform regulatory approaches to LM deployment in sensitive communication domains.
